I think I’ve got it. Tests on Appveyor and Travis are run inside a virtualenv (through Tox in Appveyor’s case), and virtualenv does not create entry points if you create a venv inside it. To reproduce:

```
$ which python3.6
/usr/local/bin/python3.6
$ python3.6 -m venv env1
$ ls env1/bin
activate  activate.csh  activate.fish  easy_install   easy_install-3.6   pip  pip3  pip3.6  python   python3
$ python3.6 -m virtualenv env2
...
$ env2/bin/python -m venv env3
$ ls env3/bin
activate  activate.csh  activate.fish  python  python3
```

You can see `pip` entry points are missing. I’m not sure what is the problem here, and if there is a workaround to it, but this is really a show-stopper for `venv`.

This error is a bug, and I'll push a fix tonight, however I should point out that you are retrieving the ticket in a way that I did not intend. The correct way to retrieve a ticket is:

```python
ticket  = zenpy.tickets(id=38884)
```
The `get_ticket` method is actually used internally by other objects that have a ticket property and is not meant to be used directly. I can see that it's presence in the ticket api is confusing to users so I might make it a private method to make it more clear it is part of Zenpy's internals.

@reenberg, a few more thoughts:

- If your project is large and is already using a build system, you could take advantage of external CSV (those specified with a path in the markdown) and write another Python script to create the subcell CSV files, which is to be included in the markdown through the path. The advantage of this is that pantable can remains simple and leaves the infinite configurability through another independent script (i.e. each tool do one thing and one thing good).

- Alternatively, there might always be some advanced functionality that could be done, but its syntax might be too complicated. We could hide these functionalities inside a key so that normal users are not confused with the syntax. Furthermore, these advanced features can be said to be experimental to allow faster prototyping without committing into it.

I have another feature I had in mind (that generates plots from CSV) that share the same problem with this feature (i.e. syntax seems to complicated and more like programming than being markdown-ish).

What happens if you start ZAP manually using the command in your output? i.e.
```
/home/ZAP_D-2018-03-05/zap.sh -daemon -port 8030 -config api.key=12345

Closing this issue with some final thoughts:

Lazy rendering
--------------------

Duplicating the ``initscr()`` round of functions in curses is beyond the scope of Blessed. Very much of what is discussed in these first two points are relative to such features in the curses library (such as lazy painting).

On FmtStr
--------------

We wish very much return only Unicode strings, to have deterministic output given input, and a limited amount of state.  As for combining, Please see #61 which enables nested styling.


On querying cursor position
-------------------------------------

Yes, will merge soon.

On UI
--------

such as readline, dialog boxes, progress bars, and other fun.


Blessed is *only* a very thin and simple to use API abstraction layer above curses, providing a pythonic interface over really, only, the simplest of sequence emission and parsing that curses provide. As an example, Blessed as an API is comparable to its matching graphical API's provided by the Linux Framebuffer or OpenGL: low-level pixel-pushing kind of libraries.
To ensure scope is limited, blessed will not attempt to solve abstractions of curses that would occur after ``initscr()``, or any facsimile UI interfaces.  This is important to ensure it is a "core" dependency of any upper layer API's that are welcome to provide such interfaces.

On keyboard callbacks/event loops
-----------------------------------------------

[Currently, blessed provides a perfectly functional API for those wishing to use an asyncio event loop -- and this is very much the purpose of the design of python 3.4 asyncio: to allow asynchronous I/O by without enforcing a particular event looping paradigm.

I have not yet provided an example of blessed used with asyncio in the bin/ folder -- we need to find an asyncio project to integrate with and so. An example I have written using stdin as a polling callback: https://github.com/jquast/telnetlib3/blob/fd39251b7650f4834b43891b9a5020845dfa197e/bin/telnet-client#L59 -- such function could easily be replaced in curtsies or any other UI system with a call term.inkey(timeout=0), and methods such as ``getch`` and ``kbhit`` may be derived and modified to accomidate any event looping system you wish.  Enforcing a callback mechanism is out of scope, however.

asyncio can be instructed to perform a callback when a filedescriptor (stdin) is ready, which you could then author your own coroutine to poll term.inkey(timeout=0.0) -- which may be u'' if only a partial sequence was received up to this moment.

Now that your cache ready, let's write a simple rule:  

1. Start a terminal  
2. Create a directory for your app: `mkdir firstapp` `cd firstapp`  
3. In the new directory `sudo pip install durable_rules` (this will download durable_rules and its dependencies)  
4. In that same directory create a test.py file using your favorite editor  
5. Copy/Paste and save the following code:
  ```python
  from durable.lang import *
  with ruleset('test'):
      @when_all(m.subject == 'World')
      def say_hello(c):
          print ('Hello {0}'.format(c.m.subject))

      @when_start
      def start(host):
          host.post('test', {'id': 1, 'sid': 1, 'subject': 'World'})

  run_all()
  ```
7. In the terminal type `python test.py`  
8. You should see the message: `Hello World`  

Note: If you are using [Redis To Go](https://redistogo.com), replace the last line.
  ```python
  d.runAll([{'host': 'hostName', 'port': port, 'password': 'password'}]);
  ```

Lag `k` sets the time back from `t` to `t-k`. This would be helpful if you were looking at data that gets revised and you want to wait till the revised number comes out so you can act on it. For example, today the Initial Jobless Claims from last week was revised to 223k. So to incorporate this data you could lag the Initial Jobless Claims by 1 week.

I finally wrote a hack. Now I can open TDMS files as large as 5GB (more than 4k records per file), jump to the desired position, read desired number of points and return them, without sequentially reading the files.

This is a part of a GUI tool I wrote that that visualizes TDMS files (but also other IQ formats, even WAV files). The code can be found also [here](https://github.com/xaratustrah/iq_suite) on github.

The core idea is to read the first 2 records of the file to estimate sizes that are needed for `f.seek()` along with some other constants. Then in any future run, one can jump as many records as needed.

To determine how many records one needs to read, from a given `starting_sample`, `number_of_samples` I calculate how many full records need to be read. I read those records from the file but return only the desired samples in a simple `numpy complex64` array. This way the whole TDMS thing is transparent to the user.

The whole implementation is just 2 functions withing the file [iqdata.py](https://github.com/xaratustrah/iq_suite/blob/master/iqdata.py), and when removing comments and debug info, it shrinks to a couple of lines. The two functions are called `read_tdms_information` and `read_tdms`, which are used for the first initialization and future reading respectively as described above.

But unfortunately I am not using npTDMS, but just calling the `read_segment` from the original [pyTDMS](http://sourceforge.net/projects/pytdms/files/?source=navbar) of Floris van Vugt **absolutely without** any change.

If you are interested, I would be happy to help, somehow build this simple trick also into npTDMS. There are no dependencies nor other complex structures, just subtle looping and conditional jumps.

**TL;DR**: That's the correct behavior.  Use `quaternion.rotation_intrinsic_distance(q1, q2)` to get the angle between two __rotations__, instead of between two quaternions.  (But try to remember that while quaternions uniquely specify rotations, rotations do not uniquely specify quaternions.)

You are running into the fact that quaternions are ["spinorial"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spinor), which means — for example — that a quaternion that rotates by 90° is *not the same* as a quaternion that rotates about the same axis by -270°, or about the opposite axis by +270°.  Similarly (but perhaps more surprisingly), a quaternion that rotates by 360° is not the same as a quaternion that rotates by 0°; you have to go through a full 720° to get back to where you started in quaternion space.

Obviously, this is not the case with rotation matrices; they are not spinorial.  But this means that there is not a unique quaternion to represent any given rotation matrix; there are actually two, and they just differ by a negative sign.  To see why, write down the formula to rotate a vector `v` by some quaternion `Q`:

    vprime = Q * v * Q.conj()

Notice that there are two places `Q` appears, so we can just flip its sign without affecting the result:

    vprime = (-Q) * v * (-Q).conj()

It turns out that when you rotate a quaternion by 360°, it gives you back precisely the negative of that quaternion.  In fact, technically you're going even farther because you're not just using the conjugate in the formulas above; you're actually normalizing your quaternions, which means that you could multiply the quaternion by *any* nonzero number (not just -1), and get the same rotation.  I'm assuming that the quaternions are already normalized, so they're technically ["rotors"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rotor_(mathematics)), which is a term you see in several of the functions in this package.

So any time you ask "What quaternion will give me the same rotation as this rotation matrix?", there are actually *two* correct answers, which just have opposite signs.  Thus, when `pyquaternion` gives `Q1=[0, 1, 0, 0]` while `quaternion` gives `q1=[0, -1, 0, 0]` for your chosen rotation matrix, we're both right.  (And from what you've shown me at least, I don't see any reason to think that `pyquaternion` uses different conventions than `quaternion`.  But we evidently have different algorithms for choosing a quaternion based on a rotation matrix.)

You may wonder why any functions bother being sensitive to this sign difference when it doesn't make any difference *for a rotation*.  The answer is that it makes all the difference in the world when you're manipulating multiple quaternions.  For example, if you have a quaternion function of time and you want to differentiate it, you want the function to be pretty smooth.  But if, for example, my functions always chose the "smallest" equivalent rotation angle, the quaternion function would eventually get to a point where I would suddenly have to jump to a totally different quaternion (the negative), which would make the derivative totally crazy.  Same deal with interpolation by `squad` and `slerp`; a physical object would barely rotate between those two time steps, but the interpolated quaternion would suddenly spin through almost 360° for no reason.  So except in a few very specific cases, the functions in any good quaternion package have to respect those differences.

Finally, you may say "That's all well and good, but I just care about a few discrete rotations, and I just want the rotational angle, not the spinorial angle.  Isn't there a function that just gives me that?"  The answer is yes:

```python
def angle_error_q(rots1, rots2):
    q1 = quaternion.from_rotation_matrix(rots1)
    q2 = quaternion.from_rotation_matrix(rots2)

    angles = np.array([quaternion.rotation_intrinsic_distance(r1, r2)
                       for r1, r2 in zip(q1, q2)])

    return rad2deg(angles)
```

To fix this, turn them into explicit errors by adding a filter somewhere near the root of your tests... and prepare yourself to shave some yaks :smile:

Modules do not have a `__qualname__` attribute:

```python
>>> from qualname import qualname
>>> import sys
>>> sys
<module 'sys' (built-in)>
>>> sys.__qualname__
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-5-262cc5ea0da1>", line 1, in <module>
    sys.__qualname__
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute '__qualname__'

>>> qualname(sys)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-6-d008005da19b>", line 1, in <module>
    qualname(sys)
  File "/Users/wbolster/.virtualenvs/tmp-fb21cafaa4f02d6/lib/python3.4/site-packages/qualname.py", line 50, in qualname
    return obj.__qualname__  # raises a sensible error
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute '__qualname__'
```

`qualname(x)` is the same as `x.__qualname__`  on recent Python 3 versions The whole point of this library is that it is an emulation of that behaviour for older Python versions, particularly Python 2.7 which does not support this natively.

Oh BTW, what I meant by:

> Then I got deeper and found out that libketama indeed have collisions but we don't see it because of its ring implementation

The libketama ring implementation is to regenerate the ring when the node list changes! Which is exactly what we're trying to run away from ;)

This means that there is no way to retain ketama compatibility with our goal of a performance steady ring topology change.

So we need to abstract the topology change logic from the ring implementation when the ketama algorithm is used.

Fixed issues with the coverage tests and took a first draft for the docs. While writing this I ran into a bit of a issue with the environment variable idea. 

To define profiles I need to import settings, which already loads in the profile from the environment. 

So to address this I added a function that loads the profile defined in the environment. Though honestly it may be better to remove this function and just document this pattern for loading in a profile from the environment.

```
>>> from hypothesis import Settings
>>> Settings.register_profile("ci", Settings(max_examples=1000))
>>> Settings.register_profile("dev", Settings(max_examples=10))
>>> Settings.register_profile("debug", Settings(max_examples=10, verbosity=Verbosity.verbose))
>>> Settings.load(os.getenv(u'HYPOTHESIS_PROFILE', 'default'))

An excellent question!

If you're only going to use Django, then there's no real advantage over
using any other wsgi server. A few minor advantages include:

- Pretty decent async static file server via aiohttp
- Ability to schedule background tasks as coroutines from the Django app
using loop.create_task()

However, the main reason this library exists is that it allows you you run
Django alongside an aiohttp application, allowing you to implement critical
pieces of your app as async, but keep most of it inside Django.

> Are the bodies of two the same post requests the same?

They're semantically the same. `a=foo&b=foo` and `b=foo&a=foo` are *semantically* the same. Take a look at this example:

```py
>>> r = requests.post(url, data={'a': 'b', 'c': 'd', 'e': 'f'})
>>> r.request.body
'a=b&c=d&e=f'
```

That value (`a=b&c=d&e=f`) is exactly the value that betamax stores in a cassette. The next time I make that request this happens:

```py
>>> r = requests.post(url, data={'a': 'b', 'c': 'd', 'e': 'f'})
>>> r.request.body
'c=d&e=f&a=b'
```

Here are some reasons why we don't compare dictionaries:

```py
requests.post(url, json={'foo': 'bar'})
with open('afile.txt', 'rb') as fd:
    contents = fd.read()
requests.post(url, data=contents)
requests.post(url, data={'foo': 'bar'}, files={'biz': 'baz', 'bar': 'abcde'})
```

We can't turn each of those into dictionaries. And in your case, the `Content-Type` would be misleading as to whether or not we could (which is what we would have to inspect in order to determine how to parse a body into a dictionary, and a dictionary also isn't the only data structure that a body could be parsed into).

> As I see betamax implicitly changes the order of the parameters while serializing the body.

I assume you haven't read the code. If you had, you'd see we do not modify the body. We will store it converted as a base64 encoded blob when asked (or in certain other special cases) but we never modify the body. So, no, we're not implicitly changing the order of anything when we serialize the body.

> But my point is that inside match recorded_request['body']['string'] and request.body are the same except for the order when I do the same POST request.

Correct. That happens anyway, with or without betamax. This happens in requests. Inspect the `response.request.body` attribute to see it. If you're on Python 2, you'll have a harder time seeing it inside the same interpreter session, so you'll want to make a script to print that information and run it twice.

If you don't want to maintain a special matcher for your code, you can do the following

```py
r = requests.post(url, data=[('foo', 'bar'), ('biz', 'baz')])
```

As long as the data is always in that order, it will always match because requests will always serialize it in that exact order:

```py
>>> r = requests.post(url, data=[('a', 'b'), ('c', 'd'), ('e', 'f')])
>>> r.request.body
'a=b&c=d&e=f'
>>> r = requests.post(url, data=[('a', 'b'), ('c', 'd'), ('e', 'f')])
>>> r.request.body
'a=b&c=d&e=f'
>>>
```

@gvanrossum: If I understand it correctly, I think the point with deepcopy is that if you have code like:
```
from dataclasses import *
import copy

# create a parent/child relationship, child has a dict field
@dataclass
class Child:
    d: dict

@dataclass
class Parent:
    child: Child

orig = Parent(Child({1:2}))

# create a recursive copy, but just copy fields
recursed = asdict(orig, nested=True, copy_function=None)

# these two are the same: the dict in the recursed 'child' is the same as the original object
print(orig, id(orig.child.d))
print(recursed, id(recursed['child']['d']))

# now create a deepcopy of the recursed object
deep = copy.deepcopy(recursed)

# and its child's dict is now different
print(deep, id(deep['child']['d']))
```
This produces the output:
```
Parent(child=Child(d={1: 2})) 4293494464
{'child': {'d': {1: 2}}} 4293494464
{'child': {'d': {1: 2}}} 4293194784
```
The first line is the `orig` object, the second one is the `recursed` copy, and the last one is the deepcopy of `recursed`. The first two share the child "d" member, the last one has made a copy.

So, it seems to me that we should always just assign fields (no copying), and always just recurse, relying on `copy.deepcopy()` to do any needed copying.

I'm not sure how we'd share the concept of "this is a dataclass" with "this is an attrs object", but I'll have to think about it.

> Isn't finding the parent template just iterating trough the parents?

In theory, yes, but the graph structure isn't represented this way so I wouldn't implement it that way.

This is how I would do it:

```python
def find_containing_template(code, link, names):
    """Return the template matching *names* in *code* that contains *link*."""
    for template in code.filter_templates(recursive=True):
        if template.name.matches(names) and link in template:
            return template
    return None
```

Example:
```python
>>> c = mwp.parse("blah blah <ref>{{cite web|url={{quote|text=http://example.com}}}}</ref> blah blah")
>>> names = ["cite web", "cite book", "whatever"]
>>> t = find_containing_template(c, "http://example.com", names)
>>> print t
u'{{cite web|url={{quote|text=http://example.com}}}}'
>>> c.insert_after(t, "{{dead link}}")
>>> c
u'blah blah <ref>{{cite web|url={{quote|text=http://example.com}}}}{{dead link}}</ref> blah blah'
```

django-reversion is designed to allow a complete record of model changes to kept and rollback to any previous state to be carried out. This allows the "trunk" model version to be a standard model instance, with all the useful database integrity checks this provides.

True moderation is a complex problem, particular so when database relations and integrity checks are involved. There's also the social systems of requesting approval that need to be put in place. In other words, it's a difficult problem that needs to be baked into an application's architecture from the start.

Reversion is a bolt-on general purpose module that aims to fit 90% of use cases. For everything else, thats why they pay us developers the big bucks! :P

@erwagasore @joaobarbosa @eriktaubeneck 

If you are going to use factory method, recursion error will raise if it is not used properly. Here is the example based on author's example for your reference. The trick is using the ```state``` object returned by ```init_app``` for setting cutomized mail sending function. The author has explained it [here](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/issues/141#issuecomment-21728482)  and [here](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/issues/141#issuecomment-21744531) in #141.

```python
from celery import Celery
from flask import Flask
from flask_security import Security, .. datastore stuff ..
from flask_mail import Mail

celery = Celery()
mail = Mail()
security = Security()

def create_app():
    app = Flask(__name__)
    mail.init_app(app)
    security_ctx = security.init_app(app,..datastore stuff..)

    @celery.task
    def send_flask_mail(msg):
        mail.send(msg)

    # Flexible way for define custom mail sending function.
    @security_ctx.send_mail_task
    def delay_flask_security_mail(msg):
        send_flask_mail.delay(msg)

    # You can also define like this as shortcurt
    security_ctx.send_mail_task(send_flask_mail.delay)

    return app
```

I think it's fine the way it works now, you just have to keep track of the type of objects you're using. Remember that the ORM layer treats a `Sedan` and its `Car` as two *separate* objects that are not equal to each other.

Here's a full snippet that runs your example above against the database, and then does an equivalent test against a `MockSet`. Play around with it, and let me know if it makes sense. The patch I'm working on will help manage all the relationships between models so you don't have to think as much about this kind of stuff.

    # Tested with Django 1.9.2
    import logging
    import sys
    
    import django
    from django.apps import apps
    from django.apps.config import AppConfig
    from django.conf import settings
    from django.db import connections, models, DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS
    from django.db.models.base import ModelBase
    
    from mock import patch, PropertyMock, MagicMock
    
    from django_mock_queries.query import MockSet
    
    NAME = 'udjango'
    
    
    def main():
    
        setup()
    
        class Manufacturer(models.Model):
            name = models.CharField(max_length=25)
    
        class Car(models.Model):
            make = models.ForeignKey(Manufacturer)
            model = models.CharField(max_length=25, blank=True, null=True)
            speed = models.IntegerField()
    
            def format_speed(self):
                return '{0} km/h'.format(self.speed)
    
        class Sedan(Car):
            pass
    
        syncdb(Manufacturer)
        syncdb(Car)
        syncdb(Sedan)
    
        make = Manufacturer.objects.create(name='aston')
        c1 = Sedan.objects.create(speed=50, make=make)
        c2 = Car.objects.create(speed=100, make=make)
        assert Car.objects.count() == 2
        assert c1.speed == 50, c1.speed
    
        sedans = list(Car.objects.filter(sedan__isnull=False))
    
        assert c2 not in sedans  # Makes sense.
        assert c1 not in sedans  # Surprising.
    
        # Passes when using super_ptr: i.e. car_ptr
        assert c1.car_ptr in sedans
    
        # Try again with clearer variable names.
        sedan3 = Sedan.objects.create(speed=50, make=make)
        car4 = Car.objects.create(speed=100, make=make)
        assert Car.objects.count() == 4
        assert sedan3.speed == 50, sedan3.speed
    
        # This is a list of Car objects, not sedans.
        sedan_cars = list(Car.objects.filter(sedan__isnull=False))
    
        assert car4 not in sedan_cars  # It was filtered out.
        assert sedan3 not in sedan_cars  # It's not a Car object.
    
        assert sedan3.car_ptr in sedan_cars
    
        # Hopefully that all makes sense, so let's try it with MockSet.
        logging.info("Starting mock mode, shouldn't see any SQL after this.")
        with patch.object(Car, 'objects', MockSet()) as mock_cars, \
                patch.object(Sedan, 'car_ptr', None):
            sedan5 = Sedan(speed=50)
            sedan5.car_ptr = sedan5.sedan = sedan5
    
            car6 = Car(speed=100)
            mock_cars.add(sedan5.car_ptr, car6)
            assert Car.objects.count() == 2
            assert sedan5.speed == 50, sedan5.speed
    
            sedan_cars = list(Car.objects.filter(sedan__isnull=False))
    
            assert car6 not in sedan_cars  # It was filtered out.
            # This is different, but it hasn't caused me problems.
            assert sedan5 in sedan_cars
    
            assert sedan5.car_ptr in sedan_cars
    
        # Usually, I don't need both directions, so I keep it simple.
        with patch.object(Car, 'objects', MockSet()) as mock_cars:
            sedan7 = Sedan(speed=50)
            sedan7.sedan = sedan7
    
            car8 = Car(speed=100)
            mock_cars.add(sedan7, car8)
            assert Car.objects.count() == 2
            assert sedan7.speed == 50, sedan7.speed
    
            sedan_cars = list(Car.objects.filter(sedan__isnull=False))
    
            assert car8 not in sedan_cars  # It was filtered out.
            # This is different, but it hasn't caused me problems.
            assert sedan7 in sedan_cars
    
        logging.info('Done.')
    
    
    def setup():
        DB_FILE = NAME + '.db'
        with open(DB_FILE, 'w'):
            pass  # wipe the database
        settings.configure(
            DEBUG=True,
            DATABASES={
                DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS: {
                    'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.sqlite3',
                    'NAME': DB_FILE}},
            LOGGING={'version': 1,
                     'disable_existing_loggers': False,
                     'formatters': {
                        'debug': {

                                      '%(name)s.%(funcName)s(): %(message)s',
                            'datefmt': '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'}},

                        'console': {
                            'level': 'DEBUG',

                            'formatter': 'debug'}},

                        'handlers': ['console'],
                        'level': 'INFO'},
                     'loggers': {
                        "django.db": {"level": "DEBUG"}}})
        app_config = AppConfig(NAME, sys.modules['__main__'])
        apps.populate([app_config])
        django.setup()
        original_new_func = ModelBase.__new__
    
        @staticmethod
        def patched_new(cls, name, bases, attrs):
            if 'Meta' not in attrs:
                class Meta:
                    app_label = NAME
                attrs['Meta'] = Meta
            return original_new_func(cls, name, bases, attrs)
        ModelBase.__new__ = patched_new
    
    
    def syncdb(model):
        """ Standard syncdb expects models to be in reliable locations.
    
        Based on https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.9.3
        /django/core/management/commands/migrate.py#L285
        """
        connection = connections[DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS]
        with connection.schema_editor() as editor:
            editor.create_model(model)
    
    main()

Hi:

We recently investigated this and came up with a sort of solution. Its
not as automated or flexible as django's migrations or south,but it
worked for us, and is better than the alternative.

Our first conclusion was that the existing migration tools are
unusable with cassandra - too much sql and too relational.

 cqlengine/cassandra can't generally alter the types of existing
columns, neither can it add/alter/drop columns with a key. The only
way to sync these is to drop and re-sync the model. This of course
loses data.

Our solution was to develop a framework that allowed existing data to
be backed up to a tempoary table, then migrated and restored after the
model is dropped and re-synced to the keyspace.  Key to this is a
db_model that is reverse engineered from the current cassandra column family. This allows the existing data to be queried and backed up.

Migration scripts are written by developers and applied manually. This
framework doesn't generate migrations , neither does it track the
state of the db, and has no history of previous migrations. Migrations
cannot be undone.

Its a bit of a hack, but I think the real solution lies within
django-nonrel.

Putting aside the notion that the 'total_count' can change while paging... in the two code snippets I don't believe the logic for advancing **offset** was correct. 

```python
offset += len(items)
```
should be:
```python
offset += limit
```

The [V2 API for Get Items](https://box-content.readme.io/reference#get-a-folders-items) state:

> **offset**	StringURL Parameter (default=0)
> The offset at which to begin the response. An offset of value of 0 will start at the 
> beginning of the folder-listing. Note: If there are hidden items in your previous
> response, your next offset should be = offset + limit, not the # of records you
> received back.

How about introducing batch-groups? A batch-group would be an entity between the batch-set and its contained batches. A batch-set (like the `train` one) could contain a method named `batch_groups = batch_set.get_batch_groups(max_batch_size_in_bytes)`. The array `batch_groups` would then contain batch group objects. Each of them would represent a series of batches that all conform to the memory limit `max_batch_size_in_bytes`. However: Each of them have different (descending) batch-sizes. So, for example, a call for groups with memory batch sizes of 4MB like `batch_set.get_batch_groups(4M)` would result in a batch-group list like this (k=1000, M=1000k):
- batch group 1: 500 batches of batch size 400, where 10k (largest sample) * 400 = 4M
- batch group 2: 300 batches of batch size 200, where 20k (largest sample) * 200 = 4M
- batch group 3: 250 batches of batch size 100, where 40k (largest sample) * 100 = 4M
- batch group 4: 100 batches of batch size 10, where 400k (largest sample) * 10 = 4M

so here's how i implemented this so far:

https://0xacab.org/monkeysphere/monkeypy/commit/0bf0d9f30b419bdccbb1d4942665124294730a88#6768269555ca74f15c1cdba28cf1d4a0b31feb67_43_69

instead of relying on a blind `is_revoked` property (from #223 - obviously too naive), we expand the check as discussed to cover revoker delegations and so on. in short, this would look like:

```python
def is_revoked(subkey, primary=None):
  revoker = primary or subkey
  for sig in key.revocation_signatures(): # needs #227
    if sig.signer != revoker.fingerprint.keyid:
      continue
    verified = revoker.verify(key, sig)
    if verified:
      return verified
  return False
```

... and that's just for self-signatures. for revoker delegation, we'll probably need to turn that into a set of revokers and dynamically load the public key material for those folks in my program.

@kmike @waqasshabbir I honestly don't understand why, from a design perspective, you're adding in this needless complexity to begin with. It seems to me that the right way to do it is to return time zone aware datetimes *if* the string you parsed has a time zone in it, and otherwise return a naive time zone.  One option like, `IGNORE_TIME_ZONE` or something would allow people to specify that you want a naive object with time zone ignored, and then all other time zone logic is handled by the end user. Allowing user-specified lists of string <-> zone mappings is also probably a good idea.

If you do something where you are handling *all* time zone applications as part of the parsing step, people will be confused as to how the time zones are being treated, and you'll end up with a bunch of complicated options for how to deal with different cases using different zones - all of which can easily be handled by the user just manipulating the results using python's (superior) datetime functions.

> Indexing a DataArray to get a DataFrame is still odd for me.

Try `da.loc[:, :, "GOOG"].to_pandas()`

The `to_dataframe()` method is [designed for tidy data](http://xarray.pydata.org/en/stable/pandas.html#hierarchical-and-tidy-data). On a DataArray, it essentially calls `da.to_series().to_frame()`. You want the API for [preserving multi-dimensional data](http://xarray.pydata.org/en/stable/pandas.html#multi-dimensional-data).

The importance with cash is the following:
In https://github.com/quantopian/pyfolio/blob/master/pyfolio/pos.py#L22 we divide positions by `position.sum()`. Naively, if you had $100 long and $-100 short you would have 0 in the denominator and get infinity as a result. The odd thing with shorting is that it shows up as cash. Thus, if you were $100 long and $-100 short you would have $100 cash (from the short position) and the denom would be 100 and you'd be %100 long and %100 short. In your test you have a long-only portfolio so the problem does not occur, you can just set the cash column to 0 in that case.

You could specify the path to external h2o jar there following by the driverif configuration since it will get expanded to the full command line which is used to start H2O on yarn.

So for example, what you could do is: `conf.setH2ODriverPath("/Users/foo/path/to/exteded.jar -J -driverif -J YOUR_IP")` the `-J` is important for the correct interpretation of the command on the driver side.

Hope that helps!

Le fichier datatrees.py

bf30b1fcb888c06b/openfisca_france/model/datatrees.py#L37> , qui stocke les
catégories dans lesquelles sont rangées les variables d'entrée, connaît
uniquement les variables d'entrée. Et la vraie variable d'entrée du point de
vue du système socio-fiscal, c'est le salaire de base. Les autres salaires
sont calculés, mais comme tu le sais on peut les court-circuiter en leur
fournissant une valeur, auquel cas le moteur ne les calcule pas.

chaud la variable salaire_de_base en salaire_imposable, après avoir
téléchargé le JSON depuis ton application.
* 
* Ou alors tu souhaites calculer des charges sociales et dans ce cas, si tu
veux, tu actives la réforme d'inversion de revenus comme documenté ici
<https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-web-api/blob/next/docs/reforms.md#te
st-if-it-works> . Tu devras aussi modifier à chaud la variable

I pushed batch size support in reshape (and in Dim in general).
The code now works with the following change:
```python
from dynet import *

m = Model()
lp = m.add_lookup_parameters((100, 10))

# Add parameters
W = parameter(m.add_parameters((5, 10)))
b = parameter(m.add_parameters((5)))
builder = LSTMBuilder(1, 10, 10, m)

# Batch lookup (for every timestamp of the LSTM)
batch = [(1, 2, 3, 4, 5), (5, 4, 3, 2, 1), (1, 1, 2, 2, 3)]
minibatch_size = 3
seq_length = 5
inputs = [lookup_batch(lp, [batch[i][t] for i in range(minibatch_size)]) for t in range(seq_length)]

# Apply some transformations
lstm_outputs = builder.initial_state().transduce(inputs)[-1]
lstm_outputs = transpose(reshape(lstm_outputs, (10,), batch_size=minibatch_size)) # reshape to (minibatch_size, 10)
output = colwise_add(W * transpose(lstm_outputs), b)

print 'output.npvalue().shape =', output.npvalue().shape, '(batch_size=3, output dimension=5)'

# Using pickneglogsoftmax_batch (fails with "what():  Bad input dimensions in PickNegLogSoftmax: [{5,3}]")
labels = [1, 2, 3]
print pickneglogsoftmax_batch(output, labels).npvalue()
```

Regarding how to add an element, in `python-docx`, each new element type is represented by its own *custom element class*.

* Each custom element class is named after the XML Schema type definition it represents. For example, a `<w:r>` (run) element is defined by `CT_R` ("CT_" standing for "complex type"), so its custom element class is named `CT_R`.

* Each tag name is mapped to its custom element class in `pptx/oxml/__init__.py`. More than one tag name can be mapped to each custom element class.

* The custom element class is located with the other element classes it coheres most strongly with. This is a matter of judgement but I rarely find myself with much doubt about where one should go (or where to look for it later).

* Each custom element class subclasses `BaseOxmlElement`. This is the metaclass hierarchy that allows standard properties and methods to be defined declaratively, for example with `ZeroOrOne()` or `OptionalAttribute()`. There are a lot of examples of these throughout the `pptx.oxml` subpackage, let me know with any specific questions after exploring some of those to see the patterns.

* The metaclass provides standard methods and properties such as `get_or_add_{x}()`, `_remove_{x}()` and {x} (where `x` is the name of the class variable to which the results of say `ZeroOrOne(...)` is assigned to in a custom element class definition. More on this later if you need it.

* Custom element classes are not tested in isolation (except in certain unusual cases); rather they are tested *integrated with the code that calls them* by providing starting XML and expected XML. You'll see many examples of this in the unit tests. Look for calls to `element()` and `xml()` in the unit test fixtures; these are setting up before and after element/XML objects respectively.

It's difficult to integration test because the API streaming endpoint won't necessarily always concatenate multiple JSON objects in one chunk. It's even difficult to unit test in its current state, because `_stream_helper` is coupled to an HTTP response object.

If you can decouple the inner logic so it just operates on an IO object, then you should be able to write a unit test that passes in a mock object which emits a chunk of multiple JSON objects when read from.

Sure, I can be the maintainer of this docker image, as well the maintainer of the `edx-dl` image if possible.

About the current docker support:

- `FROM ubuntu:14.04` - Is a very old image, better use a `python` image directly (Already in this PR)
- [Multiple run commands that will create several unnecessary layers](https://github.com/coursera-dl/coursera-dl/blob/0ec980514d3d77c24486f198f631c4be7228f680/deploy/Dockerfile#L4-L7)
- [In this line](https://github.com/coursera-dl/coursera-dl/blob/0ec980514d3d77c24486f198f631c4be7228f680/deploy/Dockerfile#L12) you don't need to clone this repo, since we are in a git repo you can just add the local copy, it help if someone want to build their copy locally to test a feature, run the unit tests, or in the CI (Docker hub for example) to build the image.
- [Those lines](https://github.com/coursera-dl/coursera-dl/blob/0ec980514d3d77c24486f198f631c4be7228f680/deploy/Dockerfile#L9-L10) - is better to avoid adding anything like that, containers must be ephemeral, any information regarding authentication or keys, better to be mapped to parameters (They already are), or in the case of a keyfile, is better to keep them in a binding (`-v`).
- [This line](https://github.com/coursera-dl/coursera-dl/blob/0ec980514d3d77c24486f198f631c4be7228f680/deploy/Dockerfile#L13) is better to install the dependencies before you add the code to the repository, it optimizes the layers created by docker, so at any update, users will only have to download a layers with the size of your code, if you install the dependencies after that, you will force the users to download your code + the whole dependencies always that this repo is updated.
- The image doesn't set an entrypoint, for docker, this is the default binary that will be run when the container is started.

Another good practice is to add this image do docker hub, so any `docker pull image-name` will be enough to any docker user who wants to run this code. I already took care of this, any merge from this repo to mine, will trigger the build, if you prefer is possible to create an official group for coursera-dl where you have access, but it will require someone with access to this repository to configure the integration with docker hub. I can assist you if needed to configure this integration.

test: R()
  scenario: "eg1", "eg2"
  $res: scenario
```

and run DSC

```bash
[GW] dsc test.dsc --target test
INFO: Load command line DSC sequence: test
WARNING: while parsing a block mapping
  in "<unicode string>", line 2, column 3:
      scenario: "eg1", "eg2"
      ^
expected <block end>, but found ','
  in "<unicode string>", line 2, column 18:
      scenario: "eg1", "eg2"
                     ^
ERROR: test.dsc has caused ParserError (see above).
Please ensure there is no duplicated variable names in modules. If this is related to string with quotes please remove them, or use the 'raw()' syntax.
```

So this is intended behavior

Now remove quotes

```yaml

test: R()
  scenario: eg1, eg2
  $res: scenario
```
and run DSC

```bash
[GW] dsc test.dsc --target test 
INFO: Load command line DSC sequence: test
INFO: DSC script exported to test.html
INFO: Constructing DSC from test.dsc ...
INFO: Building execution graph & running DSC ...
DSC: 100%|████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| 2/2 [00:00<00:00,  1.86it/s]
INFO: Building DSC database ...
INFO: DSC complete!
INFO: Elapsed time 2.111 seconds.
```

and view output:

```bash
[GW] dsc-query test/test/test_1.rds -o tmp
INFO: Loading database ...
INFO: Data dumped to text files tmp.out and tmp.script.
[GW] cat tmp.out
{'res': 'eg1'}
```

So you get `res = 'eg1'`. Now:


```yaml
test: R()
  scenario: raw('"eg1"'), raw('"eg2"')
  $res: scenario 
```

run DSC

```bash
[GW] dsc test.dsc --target test 
INFO: Load command line DSC sequence: test
INFO: DSC script exported to test.html
INFO: Constructing DSC from test.dsc ...
INFO: Building execution graph & running DSC ...
DSC: 100%|████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| 2/2 [00:00<00:00,  1.87it/s]
INFO: Building DSC database ...
INFO: DSC complete!
INFO: Elapsed time 2.305 seconds.
```

it works. and 

```
[GW] dsc-query test/test/test_1.rds -o tmp
INFO: Loading database ...
Overwrite existing file "tmp.out"? (y/n/a)? a
Overwrite existing file "tmp.script"?
INFO: Data dumped to text files tmp.out and tmp.script.
[GW] cat tmp.out 
{'res': '"eg1"'}
```

Now you see it is `res = '"eg1"'`. 

So all behaviors are expected, except what you said above:

```
scenario: "eg1"
```

which drops quotes automatically. For that I do not think there is an easy fix unless we drop YAML parser completely (which we rely on very little anyways).

@wesm: yes, that is exactly what this thread is about.  packages are "hotfixed" to change their content, even with identical version and build numbers.  Only the meta-data is changed.  This is done to better constrain the dependencies.  E.g. imagine a conda package that said it worked with pandas >= 0.11.3, but then it turns out 0.14.0 breaks it -- that conda package works perfectly right up until the day pandas 0.14.0 is released and available, at which point new package plans will get the latest version of the dependencies and the package won't work, the environment will be broken.  This issue is trying to address how that situation should be resolved.

Have at times had a weird need for this feature. Our use case is also related to containers that are non-writable. Though sometimes we need to add just one more package to the environment for testing, debugging, or just something we are uncomfortable shipping in the container for whatever reason.

Expect that having some sort of stacked, partial environment would solve the issue. Stacking has been discussed in various issues, but to recap is the option to activate multiple environments in some order where all of them are used together. Partial in the sense that this environment would be incomplete. IOW it would rely on the environment it is stacked on top of to satisfy dependencies. Think these two features would be enough to scratch this itch (though I could be wrong).

This needs to start on the conda side of things.  Call it "conditional installation" or something.  When conda supports the notion of it, we can talk about how conda-build should provide metadata in packages.

We should probably deprecate `conda env attach` for the same reasons we should deprecate `conda env upload`.  `conda env upload` requires speaking the Anaconda API, which is the job of anaconda-client, not of conda.  The command should thus be `anaconda env upload` or something like that.  Just like any awareness of an Anaconda API is out of scope for conda, so is awareness of the concept of a "notebook."  The description for `conda env attach` is 

> Embeds information describing your conda environment into the notebook metadata

The dependency order is fundamentally flipped.

You can give a target length when generating a new bcolz ctable or carray.
There's an explanation in the docs, but basically every carray is split into small chunks, with the size of the chunks depending on the carray data type and the total expected length of the file.
It's good practice to give this at least the length of the file you are putting into it, but if you really want less chunks you cana even put in a much higher expected length (which will make chunks longer)

and **excluding** indices younger than 1 week old:

      source: creation_date
      direction: younger
      unit: weeks
      unit_count: 1
      exclude: True
```
Here's a truncated view of the logs you provided.  The first age filter that includes indices younger than 2 weeks old does the following:

The point of reference `1467020729`, is **Mon, 27 Jun 2016 09:45:29 GMT**
2016 weeks 23, 24 and 25 are _not_ **include**d in the actionable list because they're _not_ younger than the point of reference, which is 2 weeks ago.  This leaves weeks 26, 27, and 28.

```

Otherwise, the filter you describe would require a query, as that data does not appear in the cluster state metadata. Here's a sample of the data in the `'settings'` key:
```
{u'index': {u'number_of_replicas': u'1', u'uuid': u'GV0wgrQSSrOrcGo5kGTGNg', u'number_of_shards': u'3', u'creation_date': u'1472688005429', u'version': {u'upgraded': u'5000099', u'created': u'2030399'}, u'refresh_interval': u'5s'}}
```

This is where `creation_date` comes from, as well as the number of shards and replicas.  There is no "last touched" date in here, unfortunately.  If you wanted to write an extra filter that does queries, I suppose it could be merged.  It's not something that's on my radar, though.

If this is representative of how the `aiohttp` project treats its users (first change API, then throw "WTF"s at those who ask for the API change to be optional), then I'll make a mental note to stay clear. Wow.

1.
I don't think that FRC standard (neither old RFC 2616 no new RFC 7231-7240) specify timeout processing at all. Timeout questions are just out of scope of the HTTP RFC. There is no statement for *fetching forever* behavior as well).

2.
On other hand completely disabling all timeouts can be considered as security issue: if a server sends 1 byte per hour without breaking a connection fetching such resource effectively *hangs* an executed task.
If the server calls another (micro-)server by REST or GraphQL protocol very long timeouts should be considered as connection-lost signals, otherwise deny-of-service attack can be mastered easy.

3.
Almost every HTTP library supports timeouts configuring in API. HTTP is TCP based protocol, in turn TCP can disconnect a peer by timeout (there are several TCP options for it: `SO_KEEPALIVE` and `TCP_KEEP*` family). The problem is that options are cumbersome a little and not cross-platform.
That's why timeouts are usually calculated on application layer.

4. User should be aware about network timeouts when making HTTP requests. Documentation should be very expressive in this question. Timeouts should be mentioned in *quickstart* and explained in *advanced* in details.

5. Timeout event is pretty visible for user, explicit timeout exception is thrown (at least I believe aiohttp does it. If somewhere we've missed something -- it should be fixed).

The library is shipped as part of python batteries and the version should be 3.6.1 as well.

FYI (just updating the thread with a conversation Matt and I had), PyTorch does NOT do anything special with gradients of indexing operations. They materialize the full-sized mostly-zero gradient, and add that in directly to their equivalent of `outgrad`.

It seems this is the common case for their users (and perhaps autograd's users!), and hasn't given rise to any serious performance complaints. They're aware it's suboptimal, but it doesn't seem that it's anywhere near the top of their todo list.

So, it's not exactly a bug but a side effect of using protocol buffers. 

Protobufs don't support generic objects. One can't create an object (or a dictionary or a map) and have protobuf correctly serialize/deserialize it if the object is not defined in advance in a .proto file. But Tinode uses such generic objects in a few places. To pass a generic object through protobuf it's first converted into json then sent as an array of bytes. When you set `public` to `person@examle.com` you set it as a literal string `person@examle.com` which is not valid json and consequently server fails to parse it. There was no reporting of such errors on the server. I added it now. Send it as a quoted string `"person@examle.com"`. Then it will correctly parsed. Likewise, when you receive a generic object from server you must deserialize it from json.

Here is documentation for ```messages``` argument of ValidationError:
```
messages = None
String, list, or dictionary of error messages. If a dict, the keys will be field names and the values will be lists of messages.
```

First of all, this explanation is inconsistent in so many ways. Why would dict values only be lists? What are allowed values inside lists. It reads as "string of error messages", "list of error messages" or "dict of error messages". But "string of error messages" does not make sense. So, obviously what author wanted to say is that "string" IS an error message. And so MANY error messages could be come in the form of either lists or dictionaries. I agree that this allows putting dicts inside lists. But if you look at how (deeply)nested error messages work, they always come in form of dicts. Having two ways to report deeply nested errors (either combination of dicts and lists or just dicts all the way down to particular field where you could have either string or list of strings) makes API inconsistent and inconvenient.
Consider this:
```
{'_schema': [{'foo': {'bar': ['_schema': 'error 1']}}]}
```
and
```
{'foo': {'bar': 'error 1'}}
```
The inner schema is not there because the only reason to have it there is to be able to refer to the whole object (on top level you can not do this), so on inner levels if there is only schema level error you do not need '_schema'. If you would have an inner errors for 'bar', than you would need to have '_schema':
```
{'foo': {'bar': {'_schema': 'error 1', 'baz': 'error 2'}}}
```
The top level '_schema' is not needed since you're only reporting nested errors for top-level object.

So I think the only sane and consistent interpretation of errors structure is this: string error, list of strings (if you have more than one error for particular field) or dict of error message where keys are field names and values are again strings, lists of strings or dicts of error messages.

Yeah, adding an `only` parameter to dump to make a schema instance fully static while having the ability to serialize a subset of fields.

```
from marshmallow import fields, Schema

class Example(Schema):
    id = fields.Integer()
    name = fields.String()

data = {'id': 1, 'name': 'test'}

example = Example()
assert example.fields.keys() == ['id', 'name']

result = example.dump(data, only=['name'])
assert result.data == {'name': 'test'}
assert example.fields.keys() == ['id', 'name']
```

The reason for this is that the schema instance could be reused if this functionality existed, and my application provides the ability to request a subset of fields for a resource on a `GET` request. The schema should still have the full field set as it is used for to load and dump on future requests that may want ALL fields (or a different subset of fields) but inherently still has all the same deserialization and serialization rules.

> One more feature, should we consider the number of special triggers such as trigger with 1 wildcard should be before trigger with 2 wildcards?

If you find an efficient way to implement this it could be fine. The sorting code is pretty gnarly already. :wink:

The overall idea of the sorting algorithm is to order the most specific triggers on top, so that the wildcards in a short trigger don't end up shadowing a better, longer trigger from matching the message instead. Things like `who is your *` would be preferable to match before a more generic `who is *` would, etc.; the current algorithm mostly works okay except in some edge cases as this ticket mentioned.

(Also, the `{weight}` tag was added as a way to manually hand-tune the priority of triggers for certain edge cases, but should be used sparingly as it actually *drastically* rearranges the sorted list, so when used on short triggers with wildcards it can cause more harm than good).

> As a side note, I'd be curious to know which layers would fail with an unexpected batch size.

I think it's only the legacy convolution, but it's hard to introduce this into a graph these days when you're running on GPU (you'd have to give `THEANO_FLAGS=optimizer_excluding=conv_dnn,conv_gemm`). For CPU, the legacy convolution is the only implementation there is for now, so there you'd always run into trouble.

Ah wait, there's also the noise layers. If the input shape is fully specified in the constructor, it will be passed on to the symbolic random generator, which allows it to pick the correct number of parallel random streams to run. If the run-time shape doesn't match the compile-time shape, it will fail.

Someone needs to write down a set of situations/use-cases where it would be useful before we can really ascertain what level of complexity is warranted.

However, having a single global session lock is probably still useful to inform the user if there's still another Ganga session running.

Other potential packages (LHCb or Atlas etc) might indeed want other services in separate environments. We could perhaps then look at a generic solution like below.

__process: services manager__

This would be responsible for registering (processes) services. Package developers would use something like servicemanager.register() to register a service in their `__init__` so the services are registered when you load in a package. This servicesmanager would start when any ganga client starts and only one would run at any given time. It could then be used to start a service (if not started) and return an RPC client to the service:
```python
dirac = servicemanager.getRPCService('Dirac')
dirac.submitjob('blah')
```
where obviously servicemanager here is bound to the RPC client for the servicemanager process.

Then there would be some obvious services that we would want to run for any ganga client instance like monitoring loop and probably a centralised job repository which as you say could have a queue of incoming jobs etc. In this scenario all repository access goes through this service so the monitoring loop service would also have something like

```python
job_repo = servicemanager.getRPCService('JobRepository')
for j in job_repo.select_jobs(status=['submitted', 'running']):
    do stuff
```

Thanks for reporting!

This is a bug in how we are specifying the columns: https://github.com/jupyter-incubator/sparkmagic/blob/master/sparkmagic/sparkmagic/utils/utils.py#L72

The reason we specify the columns at all is to preserve the order of the columns (via the ordered dict). We take the columns by looking at the keys from the first row, but the first row could have less columns if anything was `NULL`. So, this will not drop columns (because the first row has both foo and bar):

`[OrderedDict([(u'foo', 1), (u'bar', 2)]), OrderedDict([(u'foo', 3)])]`

but this will:

`[OrderedDict([(u'foo', 3)]), OrderedDict([(u'foo', 1), (u'bar', 2)])]`

A fix could be to not specify the columns, so pandas won't drop anything, and then assign the columns later. If we try to assign less columns than the dataframe has, we'll get a `ValueError`.

So, line 72 would be replaced by this:

```
df = pd.DataFrame(data_array)
for data in data_array:
    try:
        df.columns = data.keys()
        break
    except ValueError as e:
        print(e)
        pass
```

@rjdp the tradeoff is that this schema is simpler and easier to use (and implement).
But, you can't use it with Relay. If you're not using Relay this could do just fine for you...

Made some investigation, and noticed that my code was query was failing [this check](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/df2a70695e3b53d9ed7a2afb621c0d31bcda06ea/graphql/execution/base.py#L260).

My Relay's fragment was like this:
```js
fragment MyView_view on SpecificView {
   ...
}
```
and my schema, like this:
```py
class GenericViewInterface(Interface):
    id = ID()

class SpecificView(ObjectType):
    class Meta:
        interfaces = (GenericViewInterface,)
```

Changing in the Relay side to this:
```js
fragment MyView_view on GenericViewInterface {
   ...
}
```

Solves the problem, but was not the desired solution.

On the backend, changing schema to this:
```py
class GenericViewInterface(Interface):
    id = ID()

class SpecificView(GenericViewInterface):  ###    <=== Interface subclassing
   pass
```

Solves the problem, since it now passes the said checking. 

Not sure if it is a bug or I was using interfaces definition wrong (although [following the docs](http://docs.graphene-python.org/en/latest/types/interfaces/)).

Will spend some more time on this soon to make my conclusions, but any contribution is very welcome...

The python markdown package (like many others including github markdown) now defaults to not italicizing mid-word because it interferes so often with filenames using underscores. This behavior is behind an option ([smart_emphasis](https://pythonhosted.org/Markdown/reference.html#smart_emphasis)) but it defaults to True, at least currently. And when it does italicize, backslash escaping the italics should work. See e.g. using `pip install -I markdown==2.68`: ``` >>> import markdown >>> markdown.markdown("""/a/b/c_d/f_g_h_2017""") u'

/a/b/c_d/f_g_h_2017

' >>> markdown.markdown("""/a/b/c\\_d/f\\_g\\_h\\_2017""") u'

/a/b/c_d/f_g_h_2017

' >>> markdown.markdown("""_a/b_""") # italics shown u'

a/b

' >>> markdown.markdown("""\_a/b\_""") # italics escaped u'

_a/b_

' ``` So I'm at a bit of a loss why you're getting the "non-smart" behavior.

Looking into this more, I think it's best resolved by making event purging for out-of-order steps work again, which was reported separately in #803 and which I have a PR out to implement.  The graphs look weird because when the model script re-evaluates, the new data gets picked up as a continuation of the old data, even though they're using the same step sequence, resulting in the data series "zig-zagging" back to 0 on the horizontal axis and then appearing to overlay over the old data.  If you change the horizontal axis setting to relative or wall time instead of "step" then the issue is much more apparent.  Adding event purging logic should cause the old data to be purged when the new "step 1" arrives.

Note that strictly speaking this is a different condition because we're actually redoing the whole dataset from scratch, so it would be more correct to dump the entire set of data TensorBoard has loaded, not just data at a higher step count (since in theory, the new run might not start at step 1).  And that is in fact supposed to work, where the background reload process detects directory deletion and purges all the data from that directory.  However, the reload process only runs so often and so if the directory is only gone very briefly, it might not observe that happening.  This appears to be the typical case with running `python wide_deep.py` - the script deletes the `/tmp/census_model` directory but then calls logic that must recreate it very soon after, and TensorBoard usually doesn't notice.  In my testing, it did generally pick up on the `eval` subdirectory deletion, just not the `.` directory run itself.

I'm not aware of an easy way for TensorBoard to detect directory deletion even when it's very brief (if a permanent directory creation time were available, that might work, but I'm not sure that would work cross-platform), so I think it's best to just let the event purging logic handle this in your case.

Note that this could also be worked around on the user end by inserting a delay at least as long as the reload interval (by default 5 seconds, settable with `--reload_interval`) between deleting the directory and recreating it.

The communication between the Joiner Router and Joiner is always done via link-local addresses - doesn't matter how many hops away the Border Router is from the Joiner.

wpantund does not add its own link-local addresses - it simply takes them from either the NCP or linux network interface.  The NCP log clearly indicates that the link-local address is "external" (i.e. coming from the host).  Ideally, the linux host would not add link-local addresses to the wpan0 interface in the first place.  However, as a protective measure, we can have OpenThread reject adding external link-local addresses and somehow propagate that error back to wpantund.

Given that `InputIterator` is the minimal requirement for iterators in C++, I'd say it's perfectly valid to assume `==` exists. It would be trivial to work around it here, but then we need to maintain that stricter-that-the-cpp-standard policy going forward. @dbacchet This is an implementation detail of CPython itself (see [exec documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/functions.html#exec)).
The TLDR is that if exec (in python, through CAPI or with pybind) is called with a locals and globals dictionary, the code is executed as if it was inside a class definition.

Well, so I answer it myself. On install,

* certbot-auto doesn't install anything into the directory it's called from (unless you call it from a directory it would write to anyway).
* On Debian/derivates, it installs a couple of packages: `augeas-lenses libaugeas0 libc-dev-bin libc6-dev libexpat1-dev libffi-dev libpython-dev libpython2.7-dev libssl-dev python-dev python-pip-whl python-virtualenv python2.7-dev python3-virtualenv virtualenv zlib1g-dev`, resulting in about 70 MB required diskspace
* It checks for (and installs, if not present) another couple of packages. These are usually already present in most systems (many of them being "base requirements" anyway): `ca-certificates gcc python dialog`. If those were all not present (very unlikely), that would result in another ~200 MB. Considering noone runs a server without the certificates (and their openssl dependency), take away at least half of that.

That's about what I was after. Not sure if I missed something because it "ran silently", so updates to my notes are welcome.

Creating the metadata is not required at all since new solidity versions actually provide for it.

We should use the proposed methodology for contract metadata verification that solidity currently uses. There is no need to reinvent the wheel.

You can find all the details [here](https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/develop/miscellaneous.html#contract-metadata).

All the requirements you are setting and even more are already contained in the metadata file, emitted by the solidity compiler. I say even more because even the number of optimizer runs can make a difference in the bytecode result.

Of course the actual usage of the deployed metadata is something we should do and write up in raiden, but in that case the issue is going to be much simpler. We would just need to read and evaluate the metadata to determine whether the contract is valid or not but no need to create these data ourselves.
Note: this code was probably trying handle https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/2214 , but the approach was not the correct one.

From the issues you have opened my guess is that you're on a private chain that doesn't have the smart contracts deployed.

Here is what you need to check:

- make sure parity / geth is running.
- make sure the client has the json rpc interface enabled, [parity](https://github.com/paritytech/parity/wiki/JSONRPC#enabled-apis-default) [geth](https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum#programatically-interfacing-geth-nodes).
- if you're running on a [testnet](http://raiden-network.readthedocs.io/en/stable/overview_and_guide.html#firing-it-up), make sure the node is fully synchronized.
- if you're running a private chain, make sure the required smart contracts are [deployed](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/docs/smart_contracts.rst).
- make sure raiden is executed with all the proper flags for the above (`--eth-rpc-endpoint`, `--registry-contract-address`, and `--discovery-contract-address`, use `--help` for more details)

> re-try the registration
Retry doesn't seem like a good solution. The new transaction will either fail because the nonces are out-of-order (assuming the previous one was discarded), or it won't change the state of the smart contract (assuming the previous transaction was processed afterwards).

Because TPOT adheres to the scikit-learn data requirements, we do not support datasets that have a varying number of features per sample.

However, it is possible to fit such a dataset into the scikit-learn data requirements by creating a column for every feature for every sample and simply placing a missing value (`NaN`) in a column when a sample does not have a value for that feature. From there, TPOT will use median imputation to "guess" the best value for that feature when a sample is missing that value.

You can also format your dataset as described above, but manually perform the imputation yourself. If you have a better sense of a numerical value that should be present when a feature is not available for a sample, this is a better option. One common example of this approach with text data is setting the feature = 0 when a word is not present in a sentence, and setting the feature = 1 when a word is present in a sentence.

@beardypig My sincerest apologies for my comment above.  The 'problem' was due to my lack of understanding and wrong assumptions regarding Youtube's URL management flow.
As noted in the problem description above, I am using a hard-coded URL that ends with '/live' with the naive assumption that at the time a video stream is injected to Youtube, what Streamlink requires would automatically be there within a few seconds as well.  That isn't always the case, and in the 'final straw' above, what Streamlink was looking for, apparently never showed up.  In fact, that night, our website, web app, and desktop browsers did not work....only the Youtube site.
I solved the random, to no streamlink acquisition times by this hack:
Using Python:
* urllib2 to read whatever is at the '/live' URL and then store in 'content' (for example)
* regex 'content' for VIDEO_ID using re.search('\'VIDEO_ID\':\s\"(.+?)\"' then store in 'vid' (for example)
* Build a new URL: https://youtube.com/watch?v=vid
* Start a Streamlink session with the new URL using 'best' for quality.
* Even on a Raspberry Pi Zero, Streamlink acquires its needed HLS data within 10 seconds and the video begins, determinately 30 seconds later (it takes a while to buffer up).
The above is executed in an infinite loop which is blocked during video play.  After the encoder has stopped streaming, usually within a couple of minutes, Youtube will update the content at the '/live' URL and the value found at VIDEO_ID will be changed in anticipation of the FUTURE incoming stream.
The new URL is created, as noted above, and that is used, in the infinite loop, to periodically query the '/watch?v=vid' URL.
If you have ANY questions, comments, or suggestions, please know that they are very welcomed by me.
Thank you for your help and your valuable contributions to the Streamlink project!

Repentantly, Thuddwin

Just for public's sake (I know we talked about this on skype). Having is_empty() is more expressive than just checking if the `ontology` is `None`. If in the `AssocParserConfig` we default create an empty `ontol.Ontology` object, we can then guarantee it's not None, and then have a more clear `is_empty()` to replace the `None` check. I think it's generally better to have "zero" objects that reflect that object being in a default or empty state: like an empty list `[]`, empty string `""`, etc since then we communicate to the reader that it's supposed to be a `list` or `str` etc.

Some discussion on python-ideas (scroll down to point 9): https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-ideas/2017-August/046736.html

A few thoughts that could be useful (after seeing the thread here: https://mail.python.org/pipermail/async-sig/2018-January/000437.html):

First, I'd make a distinction between what "knobs" a library API might want to expose to its users. These could be things like "finish in this total amount of time no matter what (including clean-up)," "allow _x_ time for clean-up step _y_ (no matter what)," or "aim to finish in this amount of time (excluding clean-up)." Then it would be up to the library author to use that information as it sees fit. For example, if a user specified 30 seconds absolute total time and 10 seconds for clean-up, then the library would compute and allot 20 seconds for the "success" case. Or the library could even tell the user their knob values are incompatible. Lastly, it would be up to the framework (trio in this case) to expose to the library author the primitives to support using that information. One consequence of this way of thinking is that it seems like it would be best if the framework didn't even have to know about concepts like "shutdown" and "graceful." Instead, it could expose lower level operations like increasing or decreasing the allotted time, or execute this block of code within this computed amount of time, and it would be up to the library author to map those operations to support graceful shutdown, etc.

That is a nice solution to keeping `search` and `download` separate. However, I am still strongly in favour of merging both into a unified CLI command.

- the CLI should be the easy to use entry point so we have to aim to keep it simple
- `download` only fulfills the function of downloading a single scene, which could be merged into `search` or a unified CLI
- `sentinel search` is descriptive but it could cause confusion why the first part of the CLI command is `sentinel` instead of the name of the tool `sentinelsat`
- download of a single scene is often fastest dealt with by using the DHuS GUI - therefore I don't expect that `download` sees a lot of use

The downsides are:
+ spending time changing code that works
+ the proliferation of code handling different types of data structures
